agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A request to couple Sales/Order Management with Warehouse Management by blocking orders when stock is insufficient and exposing a real-time stock indicator to the sales team. This fundamentally changes the relationship between two previously autonomous bounded contexts.","bounded_context":"Cross-cutting concern spanning Warehouse Management (Context A) and Sales/Order Management (Context B). The feature as stated would violate context boundaries by making Order placement synchronously dependent on Warehouse state.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"StockLevel","definition":"An integer (>= 0) owned by Warehouse Management representing the current physical count of a Product in the warehouse."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A sales-side aggregate representing a customer's intent to purchase one or more products, owned by Sales/Order Management."},{"term":"OrderLine","definition":"A line item within an Order referencing a Product ID and a requested Quantity."},{"term":"Product","definition":"An entity that exists in both contexts but carries different meaning — in Warehouse it is an inventory item with a StockLevel; in Sales it is a catalog item with pricing and availability."},{"term":"Stock Reservation","definition":"A NEW concept that must be introduced — a temporary hold on StockLevel units claimed by an Order but not yet picked. This is the missing domain concept."},{"term":"Available Stock","definition":"StockLevel minus the sum of all active Stock Reservations. This is what the sales team actually needs — not raw StockLevel."},{"term":"Stock Availability Projection","definition":"A read-model or eventually-consistent view of Available Stock published FROM Warehouse TO Sales. Sales never queries Warehouse directly."},{"term":"Order Confirmation","definition":"A new state in the Order lifecycle where the order has been accepted AND stock has been reserved. Distinct from Order Placed."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"StockLevel in Warehouse Management cannot go below zero.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Physical constraint — you cannot ship items that do not exist. If violated, the warehouse state is corrupt and downstream fulfillment will fail.","testable_assertion":"After any operation, StockLevel for every Product must be >= 0."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Stock Reservation is owned by Warehouse Management, not Sales. Sales requests a reservation; Warehouse grants or denies it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Only the system that owns the stock state can guarantee consistency. If Sales could directly decrement stock, two bounded contexts would share write authority over the same aggregate, guaranteeing eventual corruption.","testable_assertion":"No write to StockLevel or Reservation originates from within the Sales bounded context."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"An Order cannot transition to Confirmed unless a corresponding Stock Reservation has been successfully created for ALL of its OrderLines.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Partial reservations create fulfillment nightmares — half-confirmed orders that can never ship. The reservation must be atomic across all lines or fail entirely.","testable_assertion":"Given an Order with lines for Product A (qty 3) and Product B (qty 2), if Product B has Available Stock of 1, the entire reservation is denied and the Order remains in Pending state."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Stock Reservations must have a time-to-live (TTL). Unredeemed reservations expire and release the held stock back to Available Stock.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The TTL duration is a business policy (e.g., 15 minutes, 24 hours) that can be changed by operations leadership. The existence of a TTL is critical, but the specific duration is policy.","testable_assertion":"A Stock Reservation created at time T with TTL of N minutes is automatically released at T+N if not fulfilled, and Available Stock increases accordingly."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"The 'stock available' indicator shown to the Sales team must be an eventually-consistent projection, not a synchronous query to Warehouse.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an architectural policy driven by operational resilience. A synchronous dependency would mean Warehouse downtime blocks all sales activity — an unacceptable coupling. But the specific consistency window is policy.","testable_assertion":"If Warehouse Management publishes a StockLevel change event at time T, the Sales-side projection reflects it within the configured consistency window (e.g., <= 5 seconds), and Sales can still display orders and indicators even if Warehouse is unreachable."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"The Sales context must NOT use the term StockLevel. It should use its own projected concept: ProductAvailability (e.g., InStock, LowStock, OutOfStock).","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a ubiquitous language boundary rule. Leaking Warehouse's integer StockLevel into Sales creates tight coupling and exposes warehouse internals that the sales team neither needs nor should act on.","testable_assertion":"No entity, value object, or API in the Sales bounded context contains a field named 'StockLevel' or exposes a raw integer stock count."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer places an Order with OrderLines totaling quantities within Available Stock for all referenced Products","when":"The Order is submitted","then":"A Stock Reservation is created in Warehouse Management for all lines atomically, and the Order transitions to Confirmed in Sales"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A customer places an Order where at least one OrderLine's quantity exceeds Available Stock for that Product","when":"The Order is submitted","then":"No Stock Reservation is created for any line (atomic failure), the Order transitions to Pending/Blocked, and the customer receives a clear explanation of which product(s) are unavailable"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A Stock Reservation exists for an Order that has not been fulfilled","when":"The reservation TTL expires","then":"The reserved quantities are released back to Available Stock, and the Order is transitioned to a state indicating stock hold expired"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"Warehouse Management publishes a stock change event (receipt, pick, reservation, expiry)","when":"The event is consumed by Sales","then":"The Sales-side ProductAvailability projection is updated within the configured consistency window"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"Warehouse Management is temporarily unreachable","when":"The Sales team views the order screen","then":"The last-known ProductAvailability projection is displayed (possibly stale), and the UI indicates the data may not be current — Sales is NOT blocked from operating"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"Two customers simultaneously attempt to order the last unit of a Product","when":"Both reservation requests arrive at Warehouse Management","then":"Exactly one reservation succeeds and one is denied. StockLevel never goes below zero. No overselling occurs."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Race condition: two simultaneous orders for the last unit of stock","expected_behavior":"Warehouse Management must serialize reservation requests (optimistic locking, pessimistic lock, or event-sourced) to guarantee BR-1. Exactly one order is confirmed; the other is blocked."},{"scenario":"Reservation expires while customer is completing payment","expected_behavior":"The order cannot be fulfilled. The system must re-attempt reservation or notify the customer that stock is no longer held. Payment should be refunded or not captured."},{"scenario":"Supplier receipt arrives between reservation denial and customer retry","expected_behavior":"The projection updates, customer sees availability restored, and a new order attempt succeeds. The system must not cache stale denials."},{"scenario":"Bulk order that would consume 100% of available stock for a product","expected_behavior":"Reservation succeeds if quantity <= Available Stock. However, this may trigger a LowStock event for other sales channels. Business must decide if large orders should require manual approval (policy, not invariant)."},{"scenario":"Product exists in Sales catalog but has never been stocked in Warehouse","expected_behavior":"Available Stock is effectively 0. The ProductAvailability projection shows OutOfStock. Order is blocked per AC-2."},{"scenario":"Network partition between Warehouse and Sales during order placement","expected_behavior":"The order cannot be confirmed synchronously. It should be queued as Pending and processed when connectivity is restored. The customer should be told the order is being validated, not silently accepted."},{"scenario":"Stock Reservation succeeded but the Order is subsequently cancelled by the customer","expected_behavior":"The reservation must be explicitly released, returning quantities to Available Stock. Failure to release creates phantom holds that reduce sellable inventory."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Blocking orders on insufficient stock eliminates overselling, which prevents costly backorder fulfillment, customer compensation, and refund processing. However, false negatives (blocking orders when stock actually exists but projection is stale) represent lost revenue. The consistency window and reservation TTL directly affect revenue capture.","operational":"Introduces an integration dependency between two previously autonomous contexts. Warehouse must now publish events reliably; Sales must consume them. This adds operational complexity: monitoring for event lag, handling partition scenarios, managing reservation lifecycle. Warehouse operations (cycle counts, adjustments) now have downstream sales visibility implications.","user":"Sales team gains visibility into stock, reducing manual checking and phone calls to the warehouse. Customers get faster feedback on order feasibility. However, stale projections showing 'In Stock' followed by a reservation denial will frustrate users more than no indicator at all. The UX must set correct expectations about data freshness.","risk":"PRIMARY RISK: Tight coupling disguised as integration. If implemented as a synchronous call from Sales to Warehouse, any Warehouse downtime halts all sales. SECONDARY RISK: Reservation TTL tuning — too short and customers lose holds during checkout; too long and stock is locked up reducing effective inventory. TERTIARY RISK: Eventually-consistent projection could show availability that no longer exists, leading to user trust erosion.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Faster feedback on order viability, but potential frustration if shown 'available' then denied at confirmation due to stale data or race conditions."},{"group":"Sales Team","impact":"Gains real-time-ish availability indicator, reducing manual stock checks. Must be trained that the indicator is eventually consistent, not a guarantee."},{"group":"Warehouse Operations","impact":"Must now publish inventory events reliably. Cycle counts, adjustments, and receiving all become event sources. Increased operational discipline required."},{"group":"Engineering / Platform","impact":"Must build and maintain an event-driven integration layer (event bus, projection, reservation service). New failure modes to monitor and alert on."},{"group":"Finance","impact":"Reduced overselling means fewer refunds and compensation costs. Reservation expiry must integrate with payment capture timing to avoid charging for unholdable orders."}]},"confidence":{"level":72,"reasoning":"The spec is clear about the desired outcome (block orders, show availability) but silent on critical implementation boundaries: synchronous vs. asynchronous integration, reservation semantics, consistency requirements, and TTL policy. The two bounded contexts are well-defined, but the integration pattern is unspecified, requiring me to infer the correct domain architecture. No contradictions exist, but significant ambiguity around the 'real-time' requirement and the 'automatically block' mechanism.","high_confidence_areas":["StockLevel >= 0 invariant is unambiguous","Stock Reservation as a required new domain concept","Atomic reservation across all OrderLines","Warehouse owns all stock writes"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact meaning of 'real-time' — true real-time vs eventually consistent","Whether 'block' means prevent submission or accept-then-reject","Reservation TTL duration and policy","Whether partial fulfillment is ever acceptable","Payment integration timing relative to reservation lifecycle"],"assumptions":["The business intends eventually-consistent availability, not true synchronous coupling, based on standard logistics domain patterns","Stock Reservation is the correct integration pattern rather than direct stock decrement","The feature applies to all order channels, not just a specific sales interface","Partial order confirmation (some lines reserved, others not) is NOT acceptable"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec says 'real-time stock available indicator' — in a distributed system with two bounded contexts, true real-time requires synchronous coupling which creates an availability dependency. Does the business accept eventually-consistent (seconds-stale) data, or do they literally require synchronous reads against warehouse state?","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Team","Engineering","Customers"],"options":["A) Eventually-consistent projection (recommended) — Sales has its own read model updated via events, tolerates seconds of staleness","B) Synchronous query — Sales calls Warehouse on every page load, guaranteeing freshness but coupling availability"],"recommendation":"Option A — eventually-consistent projection. It preserves bounded context autonomy, and the sales team's need for an 'indicator' does not require warehouse-grade precision."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Automatically block' is ambiguous — does this mean (A) prevent order submission entirely (front-end validation against projection), or (B) accept the order and then reject/block it after a reservation attempt fails (back-end validation against actual stock)?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Engineering"],"options":["A) Front-end blocking only — UI disables ordering when projection shows OutOfStock. Simple but can be wrong due to staleness.","B) Back-end blocking only — accept order, attempt reservation, reject if insufficient. Accurate but slower feedback.","C) Both — UI warns based on projection, back-end enforces based on actual reservation. Defense in depth."],"recommendation":"Option C — UI provides guidance based on projection, back-end enforces truth via reservation. This gives fast feedback while guaranteeing correctness."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Any implementation where Sales directly reads or writes Warehouse StockLevel","business_rule_broken":"BR-2 — Stock ownership must remain in Warehouse. Direct coupling violates bounded context boundaries and creates shared-kernel problems."},{"violation":"Any implementation where the Order is confirmed without an atomic reservation across all OrderLines","business_rule_broken":"BR-3 — Partial reservations create unfulfillable orders. All-or-nothing reservation is required."},{"violation":"Any implementation where Warehouse downtime prevents Sales from displaying orders or the availability indicator","business_rule_broken":"BR-5 — Sales must remain autonomous. A synchronous dependency on Warehouse availability is an architectural defect masquerading as a feature."}]}
